text: Every year, millions of students endure weeks of intense revision only to sit in a silent hall and pour their knowledge onto paper within a rigid time limit.
This model of assessment is not merely outdated — it is actively harmful.
Research consistently demonstrates that high-stakes exams trigger anxiety, distort learning, and reward memorisation over genuine understanding.
A persistent myth holds that exams are the sole valid measure of competence.
In reality, coursework, group projects, and oral presentations yield a far more comprehensive picture of a student's capability.
Several prestigious institutions have already adopted alternative methods and report outstanding results.
Critics contend that abolishing exams would undermine standards, but the evidence does not support this claim.
If universities truly aspire to nurture critical thinking, they must be willing to modify how they evaluate it.
Clinging to tradition for its own sake is not rigour — it is intellectual laziness.
